cataloging_subject.label: The Master of Oxford's Catechism
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The anonymous Middle English prose question-and-answer work conventionally called The Master of Oxford's Catechism, represented here by the fuller Harley MS 1304 version. The subject includes the sequence of exchanges between a clerk and master but excludes Harley MS 1304 and Lansdowne MS 762 as physical miscellanies, their distinct textual copies, the 1845, 1879, 1885, 1965, and 2023 editions or reprints, the University of Michigan encoding, and the repository XML artifact.

catalog_summary.title: The Master of Oxford's Catechism
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous
catalog_summary.date: Traditionally assigned to the early fifteenth century; exact composition and earliest-copy dates unresolved
catalog_summary.region: Place of composition unknown; the Harley copy is from England and the Lansdowne miscellany is associated with London

assertion.notes: This is the established scholarly title, not a demonstrated authorial title.

titles.value: Questiones by-twene the Maister of Oxenford and his Clerke

scope.description: Horstmann's 1885 article title and the form used for the Harley copy in the current British Library catalog

titles.value: Questions bitwene the Maister of Oxinforde and his Scoler

scope.description: Title of the shorter Lansdowne MS 762 copy in Wright and Halliwell's edition

titles.value: Sei me, where was god whanne he made heven and erthe?

scope.description: First question in the Harley version, after the speaker label
Closing formula printed by Horstmann and retained in the encoding

assertion.notes: The master and clerk are dialogue roles, not identified historical authors; no author name is supplied by the witnesses or accepted scholarship reviewed.

scope.description: The 1885 Harley MS 1304 edition used by the U-M encoding

date_statements.display: Traditionally early fifteenth century; exact date unresolved

assertion.notes: Wright and Halliwell and Wuelcker assign the Lansdowne copy to Henry V's reign, but the current British Library catalog dates the enclosing commonplace book to 1509-1564 and probably the 1520s or 1530s. The traditional date is therefore not normalized into exact years.

notes: A manuscript-copy date, even if resolved, would not by itself establish the composition date.

date_statements.display: Harley MS 1304 is securely dated to the second half of the fifteenth century; the exact date of the Lansdowne copy remains disputed

assertion.notes: Harley is securely dated 1450-1499. The older claim that Lansdowne 762 was written under Henry V could make that copy earlier, but it conflicts with current whole-volume cataloging and has not been resolved at the exact ff. 3v-5r component level.

date_statements.display: 1845 (earliest verified collected-volume edition, from Lansdowne MS 762)

scope.description: Wright and Halliwell's shorter Lansdowne version

assertion.notes: This is the earliest printed edition verified in this review. The publication history of Reliquiae Antiquae in parts was not resolved, so an earlier part-issue date remains possible.

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: The XML correctly marks TEXT/@LANG='enm'. German editorial headings and footnotes belong to Horstmann's edition rather than the medieval work.

form_statements.description: A prose dialogue of alternating questions by a clerk and answers by a master; enumerated answers sometimes span several paragraph elements.

genre_statements.label: Question-and-answer literature
notes: The sequence combines instruction with paradoxical questions, wisdom sayings, and riddling lore.

subject_statements.label: Creation, cosmology, and the natural world
Christian doctrine and the Ten Commandments

summaries.value: An anonymous Middle English prose dialogue in which a clerk questions a master through fifty exchanges. The fuller Harley version moves from creation, Adam, biblical genealogy, Noah and the Flood to cities, plants, animals, waters, fallen angels, the elements, the soul, Moses and the Ten Commandments, proverbial judgments, natural lore, and cosmological riddles. Its brief question-and-answer units combine Christian instruction with apocryphal, encyclopedic, and entertaining wisdom material.

The work survives in two British Library copies. Harley MS 1304, ff. 100r-102v, preserves the fuller version edited by Horstmann and encoded as CME00011. Lansdowne MS 762, ff. 3v-5r, preserves a shorter version first printed by Wright and Halliwell and later by Wuelcker. Modern scholarship edits the two copies in parallel and relates the work closely to the Old English Prose Solomon and Saturn, while treating their common Latin source as a reconstruction rather than a surviving witness.

extent.description: The Harley-version XML contains one DIV1 catechism with 100 alternating SP elements, representing 50 clerk/master question-and-answer exchanges. It contains 121 P, 4 PB, 2 editorial NOTE, and 1 GAP elements. The extra paragraph elements divide enumerated answers and are encoding metrics, not additional exchanges. The shorter Lansdowne version is not included in these counts.

related_works.title: The Prose Solomon and Saturn
notes: Sauer describes the texts as nearly identical in important respects, and the 2023 parallel edition reconstructs a possible common Latin source. This record does not assert that either surviving vernacular work directly derives from the other.

evidence.citation: General Corpus / CME source XML, CME00011.xml.
evidence.summary: Identifies CME00011 through IDG/@ID, BIBNO, and VID; marks the text as Middle English; and encodes Horstmann's four-page Harley-version edition with title, subtitle, fifty alternating exchanges, editorial notes, printed-page breaks, italic typography, one illegibility gap, explicit, and editor byline.
Counts 1 DIV1, 100 SP, 100 SPEAKER, 121 P, 4 PB, 2 NOTE, and 1 GAP elements. Alternating speaker labels yield fifty clerk questions and fifty master answers.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse, item CME00011.
evidence.locator: item link and collection browse citation
evidence.summary: The collection browse page identifies the anonymous item as Questiones by-twene the Maister of Oxenford and his clerke, edited by Horstmann in Englische Studien 8 (1885), and links exact item CME00011.
notes: The exact item route returned an automated Cloudflare challenge during this review; the public collection browse page and local CME artifact supplied item-level identity.

evidence.citation: Carl Horstmann, ed., 'Questiones by-twene the Maister of Oxenford and his Clerke,' Englische Studien 8 (1885), 284-287, inspected in the Johnson Reprint Corporation first reprinting (1965).
evidence.locator: original volume title page; 1965 reprint statements; contents; printed pp. 284-287 / Internet Archive leaves n294-n297
evidence.summary: The original title page dates Englische Studien VIII to 1885. Horstmann's article names Harley MS 1304, prints the fuller fifty-exchange text, notes a partly abbreviated Lansdowne 762 version in Wuelcker, marks one unreadable number in a footnote, and closes with an explicit and Horstmann's name. The scanned object is a 1965 Johnson reprint of the complete 1885 volume, not a manuscript facsimile.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Harley MS 1304, record 040-002047133.
evidence.locator: contents, physical characteristics, date, origin, access, and digitised-content status
evidence.summary: The current record places Questiones on ff. 100r-102v within a 104-folio paper codex with additional parchment and flyleaves, dates the manuscript 1450-1499, assigns origin to England, identifies adjacent works, and marks the legacy complete-object image link as currently unavailable.
notes: Its publications list gives Horstmann's volume as 1855, but the immediate volume title page and U-M browse citation establish 1885.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Lansdowne MS 762, record 040-002077106.
evidence.locator: title, contents ff. 3v-5r, compilation date, physical characteristics, origin, access, and digitised-content status
evidence.summary: The current record identifies the shorter Questions on ff. 3v-5r inside a London Tudor commonplace book probably compiled in the 1520s or 1530s, gives whole-volume dates 1509-1564, notes paper and parchment supports with ff. 1r-4v on parchment, and marks its legacy complete-object image link as unavailable.

evidence.citation: Thomas Wright and James Orchard Halliwell, eds., Reliquiae Antiquae, vol. 1 (London: John Russell Smith, 1845), 230-232.
evidence.locator: title page; The Master of Oxford's Catechism, printed pp. 230-232 / Internet Archive leaves n244-n246
evidence.summary: Prints the shorter Lansdowne MS 762 version under the supplied title The Master of Oxford's Catechism and says the manuscript was written in Henry V's reign. The current British Library whole-volume date conflicts with that old physical dating claim.

evidence.citation: Richard Paul Wuelcker, ed., Altenglisches Lesebuch, vol. 2 (Halle: Max Niemeyer, 1879), 191-194.
evidence.locator: title page; item 44, printed pp. 191-194 / Internet Archive leaves n202-n205
evidence.summary: Prints and lineates the shorter Lansdowne 762 version as a dialogue between an Oxford teacher and his pupil, identifies the manuscript, and places the text under Henry V. Horstmann cites these exact pages when distinguishing the shorter copy.

evidence.citation: The Medieval Review, review 08.12.01 of Form and Content of Instruction in Anglo-Saxon England (2007), discussing Hans Sauer's chapter on the Prose Solomon and Saturn and the Master of Oxford's Catechism.
evidence.summary: Reports that Sauer treats the two works in their textual and intellectual contexts, calls their neglected connection nearly identical, and discusses contents, structure, language, transmission, and manuscript relations.

evidence.citation: Mary F. Wheaton, 'Chapter III. Middle English: 2. Manuscript and Technical Studies,' The Year's Work in English Studies (published 3 December 2025), review of Hans Sauer and Alessia Bauer, eds., To Instruct and to Entertain (2023).
evidence.locator: extract on the first parallel edition and the detailed Narr review description
evidence.summary: Identifies Harley 1304 and Lansdowne 762 as the two Middle English copies edited in parallel, describes a tentative English-text stemma and dating/dialect discussion, and reports the side-by-side reconstructed Latin, Prose Solomon and Saturn, Harley, and Lansdowne layout with critical apparatus.

open_questions.question: When and where was the work composed, and which linguistic features belong to the work rather than the Harley or Lansdowne scribes?
next_steps: Consult Sauer and Bauer's 2023 dating, dialect, and stemmatic discussion in full and any subsequent specialist corrections.
Do not infer Oxford composition from the master's title or transfer Harley's English origin and Lansdowne's London association to the abstract work.
Resolve the older Henry V dating of Lansdowne ff. 3v-5r against current codicological cataloging before treating it as the earliest witness.

open_questions.question: What exact textual relation connects the fuller Harley copy, the shorter Lansdowne copy, the Prose Solomon and Saturn, and the reconstructed common Latin source?
next_steps: Collate the two current manuscript copies or Sauer and Bauer's complete parallel apparatus, not only their nineteenth-century editions.
Keep the reconstructed Latin source hypothetical unless a physical witness is identified.
Do not model the shorter Lansdowne version as a direct abbreviation of Harley without stemmatic evidence.

notes: The British Library catalog's 1855 date for Horstmann is a bibliographic error; Englische Studien volume 8 is dated 1885 on its title page.
The word Oxford in the conventional title identifies a dialogue role and is not used as evidence for place of composition.
The fifty-exchange count describes the fuller Harley version as represented by CME00011, not the shorter Lansdowne copy.
